comments:
This paper describes a practical system (FRDC_QA) developed for the NTCIR-11 QA-Lab English subtask that answers world-history multiple-choice questions using Wikipedia as the primary external resource. The system design is straightforward and engineering-oriented: titles and pages from an enwiki dump are stored in hash maps and Lucene indexes; named items are extracted via maximum matching; time information and redirections are indexed; and a feature-based binary classification approach is used to handle the common question type of choosing the correct/incorrect sentence. Seven interpretable features (item-item relations, text/choice/question item relativity, similarity measures to retrieved wiki articles, and a distance to negative sentences) are used to train a diverse ensemble of eleven classifiers in Weka. Other question types (temporal ordering, term selection, true/false) are handled with task-specific heuristics. The system is evaluated on the 2007 world history exam B; the authors report ~45% precision on several question types and overall 37% due to an ineffective random strategy on image-related "other" questions.

Strengths:
- Clear, implementable engineering approach leveraging Wikipedia and Lucene, appropriate for a contest setting.
- Use of multiple features that capture different forms of semantic relatedness and an ensemble of classifiers is sensible for this task.
- The paper is honest about limitations and outlines concrete future work.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Experimental evaluation is limited. The paper reports performance only on a single exam set (2007 world history B) and provides no comparison to reasonable baselines (e.g., single classifier baseline, IR-only baseline, or competing systems from the same task). The only comparison is to an expected random baseline, which is insufficient.
- Important methodological details are missing: how training and test splits were made, what training data size was used for the classifiers, hyperparameter settings, and how class imbalance (if any) was handled. No statistical significance or confidence intervals are reported.
- The ensemble aggregation method (simple averaging of predicted probabilities across very different classifiers) is ad hoc; there is no analysis or ablation showing the contribution of individual features or classifiers. An ablation study would strengthen claims about which features are most useful.
- Some feature choices are fragile or underspecified (e.g., how negative sentences are identified in Wikipedia, how stopwords are removed, and how times are normalized/extracted). The time extraction and normalization methods are not described in sufficient detail to reproduce results.
- The handling of image-based or other difficult question types by random selection undermines the overall performance and suggests lack of coverage for realistic exams; this should be made explicit earlier and minimized.
- Presentation issues: the text contains repetitions and some formatting/typo problems which reduce readability.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add stronger baselines (IR-only ranking, single classifier like logistic regression, prior published systems on the QA-Lab task) and report comparative results.
- Provide data split, training set sizes, and classifier hyperparameters; include cross-validation results or held-out test metrics and confidence intervals.
- Include an ablation study to show the contribution of each feature and of the ensemble vs. single classifiers; consider learning ensemble weights rather than simple averaging.
- Clarify and formalize heuristics such as negative sentence extraction and time normalization; report precision/recall for item/time extraction modules separately.
- Avoid random answers for image questions; if image handling is out of scope, exclude those questions from overall accuracy or clearly report per-type scores.
- Edit and proofread the manuscript for repetition and clarity.

Overall, the paper documents a sensible contest system with pragmatic engineering choices but lacks sufficient experimental rigor, comparative evaluation, and ablation analysis. With additional evaluation and clarifications the work would be more compelling.
